title: Remove duplicates from sorted array
description: Remove duplicates from a sorted array in place

examples.explanation: Initial array: [1, 1, 2], length = 3. i = 0, value = 1. j = 1, value = 1 (duplicate found). Shift elements from index 2 left: array becomes [1, 2, 2], length = 2. Recheck i = 0, j = 1 (now value = 2, no duplicate). i = 1, value = 2 (but i >= length-1, stop). Final array: [1, 2].

Start: [0,0,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4], len=10. i=0, val=0. j=1, dup (0=0). Shift indices 2-9 left: [0,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4,4], len=9. Recheck i=0, j=1 (0≠1). i=1, val=1. j=2, dup (1=1). Shift indices 3-8 left: [0,1,1,2,2,3,3,4,4,4], len=8. Recheck i=1, j=2 (1≠2). i=2, val=1. j=3, dup (1=2? no). j=4, val=2 (no dup). i=3, val=2. j=4, dup (2=2). Shift indices 5-7 left: [0,1,1,2,3,3,4,4,4,4], len=7. Recheck i=3, j=4 (2≠3). i=4, val=3. j=5, dup (3=3). Shift indices 6-6 left: [0,1,1,2,3,4,4,4,4,4], len=6. Recheck i=4, j=5 (3≠4). i=5, val=4 (i>=len-1, stop). Final: [0,1,1,2,3,4] but note duplicates at index 1-2: need another pass. Actually, after i=1, we missed dup at i=2, so algorithm continues: i=2, val=1. j=3, val=2 (no dup). i=3, val=2. j=4, val=3 (no dup). i=4, val=3. j=5, val=4 (no dup). Final array: [0,1,2,3,4] after full execution.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Use nested loops to compare each element with all subsequent elements, shifting the array left when a duplicate is found. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array Using Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete in-place removal of duplicates from a sorted array using nested loops to compare and shift elements.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach leverages the sorted nature of the array to systematically eliminate duplicates by comparing each element with all subsequent ones. Intuitively, since the array is sorted, duplicates appear consecutively, allowing us to detect them by scanning forward from each position. When a duplicate is found, we shift the entire remaining array left by one position to remove it, effectively overwriting the duplicate. This process repeats until all elements are checked. The algorithm starts with an outer loop iterating from the first to the second-last element. For each element at index i, an inner loop scans from i+1 to the end. If a duplicate is found (arr[i] == arr[j]), we shift all elements from j+1 to the end left by one, decrement the array length, and recheck the same i position because the next element has moved into j. This ensures no duplicates are missed. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops and potential shifting for each duplicate, with worst-case occurring when all elements are the same (requiring n-1 shifts of nearly the entire array). Space complexity is O(1) as modifications are in-place. Connection to L1: This level integrates the key subroutine of shifting elements upon duplicate detection, applying it iteratively across the array. Common mistakes include not adjusting indices after shifting (leading to skipped elements or infinite loops) and forgetting to handle the reduced array length. Real code pattern: Use while loops for outer iteration to allow rechecking, and for inner scanning with break conditions.

examples.trace: Initial array: [1, 1, 2], length = 3. i = 0, value = 1. j = 1, value = 1 (duplicate found). Shift elements from index 2 left: array becomes [1, 2, 2], length = 2. Recheck i = 0, j = 1 (now value = 2, no duplicate). i = 1, value = 2 (but i >= length-1, stop). Final array: [1, 2].

Start: [0,0,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4], len=10. i=0, val=0. j=1, dup (0=0). Shift indices 2-9 left: [0,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4,4], len=9. Recheck i=0, j=1 (0≠1). i=1, val=1. j=2, dup (1=1). Shift indices 3-8 left: [0,1,1,2,2,3,3,4,4,4], len=8. Recheck i=1, j=2 (1≠2). i=2, val=1. j=3, dup (1=2? no). j=4, val=2 (no dup). i=3, val=2. j=4, dup (2=2). Shift indices 5-7 left: [0,1,1,2,3,3,4,4,4,4], len=7. Recheck i=3, j=4 (2≠3). i=4, val=3. j=5, dup (3=3). Shift indices 6-6 left: [0,1,1,2,3,4,4,4,4,4], len=6. Recheck i=4, j=5 (3≠4). i=5, val=4 (i>=len-1, stop). Final: [0,1,1,2,3,4] but note duplicates at index 1-2: need another pass. Actually, after i=1, we missed dup at i=2, so algorithm continues: i=2, val=1. j=3, val=2 (no dup). i=3, val=2. j=4, val=3 (no dup). i=4, val=3. j=5, val=4 (no dup). Final array: [0,1,2,3,4] after full execution.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Shift Array Left to Remove an Element
ladders.desc: Shift all elements from a given index to the end left by one position to delete an element.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is crucial for in-place removal in the brute force approach. Intuitively, to delete an element at index j in an array, we overwrite it by moving every element from j+1 to the end one position left, effectively squeezing out the duplicate. This maintains the array's contiguous memory structure without using extra space. The algorithm takes the array, current length n, and index j as inputs. It loops from j to n-2, setting arr[k] = arr[k+1]. After shifting, the last element becomes redundant (it's duplicated), so we decrement the length by 1 to reflect the removal. Time complexity is O(n) in the worst case (shifting nearly the entire array), but when integrated into the full solution, it contributes to O(n²) overall. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called whenever a duplicate is detected in the nested loops, enabling in-place deletion. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of iterating through indices to perform shifts. Common mistakes include shifting beyond the array bounds (causing index errors) or not updating the length (leading to incorrect size). Real code pattern: Use a for loop with range(j, n-1) to safely shift.

examples.trace: Start: arr = [1,2,3,4], n=4, j=1. Loop k from 1 to 2: k=1, arr[1]=arr[2] -> [1,3,3,4]. k=2, arr[2]=arr[3] -> [1,3,4,4]. Decrement n to 3. Final: arr = [1,3,4,4], but only first 3 elements are valid.
Start: arr=[5,5,5], n=3, j=0. Loop k from 0 to 1: k=0, arr[0]=arr[1] -> [5,5,5]. k=1, arr[1]=arr[2] -> [5,5,5]. Decrement n to 2. Final: arr = [5,5,5], valid elements: [5,5].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Nested Loop Comparison for Duplicate Detection
ladders.desc: Use an outer loop to pick an element and an inner loop to scan forward for duplicates in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This core logic exploits the sorted property to identify duplicates efficiently within the brute force framework. Intuitively, in a sorted array, duplicates are adjacent or nearby, so by comparing each element with all subsequent ones, we can find duplicates without extra data structures. The algorithm involves an outer loop index i traversing from 0 to n-2 (since last element has no subsequent). For each i, an inner loop index j starts at i+1 and goes up to n-1, comparing arr[i] with arr[j]. If a match is found, it indicates a duplicate. The key insight is that after removing a duplicate via shifting, we must recheck the same i position because the next element moves into the j slot, potentially creating a new duplicate with i. This necessitates using a while loop for the outer iteration to allow i to not increment after a removal. Time complexity is O(n²) in worst case due to nested loops, with best case O(n) if no duplicates. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: When a duplicate is detected, it triggers the shift subroutine. Connection to L3: It builds on the concept of iterating through array indices. Common mistakes include incrementing i unconditionally (missing consecutive duplicates) or not breaking the inner loop after a shift (causing unnecessary comparisons). Real code pattern: Use while i < n-1 for outer loop and for j in range(i+1, n) for inner, with break on duplicate.

examples.trace: Array: [1,1,2], n=3. i=0, val=1. j=1, val=1 -> duplicate found. Stop inner loop. After shift, array becomes [1,2,2], n=2. Recheck i=0, j=1 (val=2), no duplicate. i increments to 1, but i>=n-1, stop.
Array: [0,1,1,2], n=4. i=0, val=0. j=1, val=1 (no dup). j=2, val=1 (no dup). j=3, val=2 (no dup). i=1, val=1. j=2, val=1 -> duplicate found. Stop.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Array Indices with Loops
ladders.desc: Use loops to traverse each index of an array sequentially to access or modify elements.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental for manipulating arrays in algorithms. Intuitively, arrays are contiguous blocks of memory where elements are accessed via indices (0-based). To process each element, we use loops—typically for or while—to iterate from the first index (0) to the last (n-1), where n is the array length. This allows operations like reading, comparing, or updating elements in sequence. The algorithm involves initializing a counter (e.g., i) to 0, then incrementing it until it reaches n-1, performing an action at each step. For nested iterations, an inner loop starts from the current outer index plus one to avoid redundant comparisons. Time complexity is O(n) for a single loop, O(n²) for nested loops. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: This building block enables the nested loop structure for duplicate detection. Connection to L4: It relies on basic operations like index access and increment. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., iterating beyond n-1) or confusing zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: Use for i in range(n) for single loop, or for i in range(n-1) with for j in range(i+1, n) for nested.

examples.trace: n=3. i=0: print arr[0]=10. i=1: print arr[1]=20. i=2: print arr[2]=30. Stop as i reaches n.
n=3. Outer i=0: inner j=1 -> (arr[0]=5, arr[1]=5). j=2 -> (5,6). Outer i=1: inner j=2 -> (5,6). Outer i=2: no inner as i>=n-1.

testCases.input: Array: [2, 4, 6], nested loops for all pairs
Array: [1, 1], nested loops for all pairs
Array: [3, 5, 7, 9], nested loops up to i=1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Comparing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve an element from an array using its index and compare it with another value.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is essential for array manipulation. Intuitively, an array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, each accessible via a numerical index starting from 0. Accessing involves using the index to read or write the element at that position, e.g., arr[i] gives the value at index i. Comparing elements checks if two values are equal, less than, or greater than, which is key for detecting duplicates in sorted arrays. The algorithm simply uses the index to fetch elements and a comparison operator (like ==) to evaluate. Time complexity is O(1) per access/comparison. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is performed at each step during iteration. Connection to L5: It builds on the concept of arrays as indexed collections. Common mistakes include using invalid indices (out of bounds) or confusing index with value. Real code pattern: Use arr[i] to access and arr[i] == arr[j] to compare.

examples.input: Array: [3, 7, 2], access index 1, compare with value 7
examples.output: Value at index 1 is 7, comparison: equal
examples.trace: arr = [3,7,2]. Access arr[1] -> 7. Compare 7 == 7 -> True.
arr = [5,5,6]. Access arr[0] -> 5. Access arr[1] -> 5. Compare 5 == 5 -> True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays as Indexed Collections
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is: a data structure that stores elements in ordered, contiguous memory locations accessible by indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept is prerequisite for any array algorithm. Intuitively, an array is like a row of boxes numbered starting from 0, where each box holds a value. The order is fixed, meaning elements are stored sequentially in memory, allowing fast access via their position (index). Key properties include: fixed size (in many languages), homogeneous data types (all elements same type), and zero-based indexing (first element at index 0). To work with arrays, one must understand how to declare them, initialize values, and know that indices range from 0 to length-1. This concept underpins all array manipulations because algorithms rely on traversing these indices to perform operations. Connection to L4: This foundation enables accessing elements by index. No higher-level connection as it's the base. Common mistakes include thinking indices start at 1 or misunderstanding array bounds. Real code pattern: In Python-like pseudocode, arrays are lists, e.g., arr = [1,2,3] with arr[0] = 1.

examples.trace: The array has 3 elements. Position 1 in human terms corresponds to index 0 with value 10. Position 2 is index 1 with 20. Position 3 is index 2 with 30. Length is 3, so valid indices are 0,1,2.
Single-element array. Only index 0 exists, holding value 5. Length is 1, so index 0 is valid, but index 1 would be out of bounds.

testCases.input: Array: [1, 2, 3, 4], what is value at index 2?
Array: [0, 0], what is value at index 1?
Array: [7, 8, 9], what is the first index?
Array: [5], what is the last index?
Array: [3, 5], what is index of value 5?
Array: [10, 20, 30, 40], what is index of first element?
Array: [6, 7, 8, 9, 10], what is value at index 4?

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers: one to iterate through the array and another to track the position for unique elements. Overwrite duplicates in place as you traverse.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array Using Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Complete in-place removal of duplicates from a sorted array using two pointers.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires removing duplicates from a sorted array in-place, returning the new length of the array with unique elements at the front. The two-pointer approach is optimal because it leverages the sorted property: duplicates appear consecutively. We use a 'write' pointer to track where the next unique element should be placed, and a 'read' pointer to iterate through the array. Intuition: Since the array is sorted, we only need to compare each element with the last unique element written. If the current element differs from the last written unique element, it's a new unique element, so we write it at the write pointer position and advance both pointers. Otherwise, we skip it by only advancing the read pointer. This works because we're essentially compressing the array by copying only unique elements to the front, overwriting duplicates. Step-by-step: 1. If the array is empty, return 0. 2. Initialize write = 1 (since the first element is always unique). 3. For read from 1 to n-1: a. If nums[read] != nums[write-1] (compare with last unique element written), set nums[write] = nums[read] and increment write. b. Else, do nothing (skip duplicate). 4. Return write as the new length. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the array once. Space complexity is O(1) as we modify in-place. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of comparing and writing unique elements. Common mistakes include not handling empty arrays, off-by-one errors with pointers, or modifying the array incorrectly. Real code pattern: def removeDuplicates(nums): if not nums: return 0; write = 1; for read in range(1, len(nums)): if nums[read] != nums[write-1]: nums[write] = nums[read]; write += 1; return write.

examples.trace: Initial array: [1,1,2], n=3. write=1 (index for next unique element). read=1: nums[1]=1, nums[write-1]=nums[0]=1, equal → skip, array unchanged [1,1,2]. read=2: nums[2]=2, nums[write-1]=nums[0]=1, not equal → nums[write]=nums[1]=2, write=2, array becomes [1,2,2]. Loop ends. Return write=2. Unique elements: [1,2] in first 2 positions.

Initial array: [0,0,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4], n=10. write=1. read=1: nums[1]=0, nums[write-1]=nums[0]=0, equal → skip, array unchanged. read=2: nums[2]=1, nums[write-1]=nums[0]=0, not equal → nums[write]=nums[1]=1, write=2, array: [0,1,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4]. read=3: nums[3]=1, nums[write-1]=nums[1]=1, equal → skip. read=4: nums[4]=1, nums[write-1]=nums[1]=1, equal → skip. read=5: nums[5]=2, nums[write-1]=nums[1]=1, not equal → nums[write]=nums[2]=2, write=3, array: [0,1,2,1,1,2,2,3,3,4]. read=6: nums[6]=2, nums[write-1]=nums[2]=2, equal → skip. read=7: nums[7]=3, nums[write-1]=nums[2]=2, not equal → nums[write]=nums[3]=3, write=4, array: [0,1,2,3,1,2,2,3,3,4]. read=8: nums[8]=3, nums[write-1]=nums[3]=3, equal → skip. read=9: nums[9]=4, nums[write-1]=nums[3]=3, not equal → nums[write]=nums[4]=4, write=5, array: [0,1,2,3,4,2,2,3,3,4]. Return write=5. Unique elements: [0,1,2,3,4] in first 5 positions.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare and Write Unique Elements in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that identifies unique elements and writes them to the correct position.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the two-pointer approach: iterating through a sorted array to find and place unique elements at the front. Intuition: In a sorted array, duplicates are adjacent, so uniqueness can be determined by comparing each element with the last unique element written. We maintain a 'write' index pointing to where the next unique element should go, and a 'read' index scanning the array. When we encounter an element different from the last written unique element, it's new, so we copy it to the write position and advance write. This subroutine efficiently compresses the array without extra space. Step-by-step: 1. Start with write = 1 (assuming at least one element, as the first is always unique). 2. For each index read from 1 to n-1: a. If array[read] != array[write-1], set array[write] = array[read] and increment write. b. Otherwise, do nothing (the element is a duplicate). 3. The subroutine modifies the array in-place and returns the final write index, which represents the count of unique elements. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass. Space complexity is O(1) as it uses only pointers. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called within the full solution to handle the main logic. Connection to L2: It relies on the core insight of using two pointers for in-place modification. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization of write, comparing with the wrong element (e.g., array[write] instead of array[write-1]), or not updating the array properly. Real code pattern: def uniqueWrite(nums): if len(nums) < 2: return len(nums); write = 1; for read in range(1, len(nums)): if nums[read] != nums[write-1]: nums[write] = nums[read]; write += 1; return write.

examples.trace: Input array: [1,1,2]. write=1. read=1: nums[1]=1, nums[write-1]=nums[0]=1, equal → skip, array unchanged [1,1,2]. read=2: nums[2]=2, nums[write-1]=nums[0]=1, not equal → nums[write]=nums[1]=2, write=2, array becomes [1,2,2]. Return write=2.

Input array: [3,3,3,4,4,5]. write=1. read=1: nums[1]=3, nums[write-1]=nums[0]=3, equal → skip, array unchanged. read=2: nums[2]=3, nums[write-1]=nums[0]=3, equal → skip. read=3: nums[3]=4, nums[write-1]=nums[0]=3, not equal → nums[write]=nums[1]=4, write=2, array: [3,4,3,4,4,5]. read=4: nums[4]=4, nums[write-1]=nums[1]=4, equal → skip. read=5: nums[5]=5, nums[write-1]=nums[1]=4, not equal → nums[write]=nums[2]=5, write=3, array: [3,4,5,4,4,5]. Return write=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Pointer Technique for In-Place Array Modification
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the two-pointer method to traverse and modify an array simultaneously.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves using two indices (pointers) to read from and write to the same array efficiently. Intuition: In many array problems, we need to process elements and update the array without using extra space. Two pointers allow us to separate the reading and writing processes: one pointer ('read') scans the array to examine elements, while another ('write') tracks where to place processed results. This is particularly useful when we want to filter or compress data, like removing duplicates. By moving the write pointer only when we find a valid element, we overwrite unwanted values (duplicates) with desired ones (unique elements), achieving in-place modification. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize write = 0 (or appropriate start). 2. For read from 0 to n-1: a. Check condition (e.g., if element is unique). b. If condition true, set array[write] = array[read] and increment write. c. If false, skip (read increments automatically in loop). 3. After loop, write indicates the new length or position. Time complexity is O(n) for one pass. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is specialized in L1 for duplicate removal by comparing with last written element. Connection to L3: It builds on the concept of iterating arrays with indices. Common mistakes include not initializing pointers correctly, updating pointers in the wrong order, or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: def twoPointerModify(arr): write = 0; for read in range(len(arr)): if condition(arr[read]): arr[write] = arr[read]; write += 1; return write.

examples.trace: Array: [1,2,3,4]. Condition: element % 2 == 0. write=0. read=0: arr[0]=1, not even → skip, array unchanged. read=1: arr[1]=2, even → arr[write]=arr[0]=2, write=1, array: [2,2,3,4]. read=2: arr[2]=3, not even → skip. read=3: arr[3]=4, even → arr[write]=arr[1]=4, write=2, array: [2,4,3,4]. Return write=2. Even numbers: [2,4] in first 2 positions.

Array: [5,10,15,20]. Condition: element > 10. write=0. read=0: arr[0]=5, not >10 → skip, array unchanged. read=1: arr[1]=10, not >10 → skip. read=2: arr[2]=15, >10 → arr[write]=arr[0]=15, write=1, array: [15,10,15,20]. read=3: arr[3]=20, >10 → arr[write]=arr[1]=20, write=2, array: [15,20,15,20]. Return write=2. Numbers >10: [15,20] in first 2 positions.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Arrays with Indices and Conditional Updates
ladders.desc: Learn to loop through an array using indices and update elements based on conditions.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on fundamental array manipulation: traversing an array with an index (like a for-loop) and modifying elements conditionally. Intuition: Arrays are indexed collections, and many algorithms require examining each element and possibly changing it. By using an index variable, we can access any position, compare values, and assign new values. This is a prerequisite for in-place modifications where we overwrite elements. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize a variable (e.g., index i) to start at 0. 2. Use a loop (e.g., for i in range(len(array))) to iterate through all indices. 3. Inside the loop, check a condition involving array[i] or other elements. 4. If condition is true, update array[i] or another position (e.g., array[j] = array[i]). 5. Continue until all elements are processed. Time complexity depends on loop length, typically O(n). Space complexity is O(1) if no extra arrays are used. Connection to L2: This skill is used in the two-pointer technique, where we iterate with one index and update with another. Connection to L4: It relies on basic array access and assignment operations. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds, not handling empty arrays, or incorrectly updating indices. Real code pattern: def iterateAndUpdate(arr): for i in range(len(arr)): if condition(arr[i]): # Update arr or another variable; pass.

examples.trace: Array: [1,2,3]. Loop i from 0 to 2. i=0: arr[0]=1, double → arr[0]=2, array: [2,2,3]. i=1: arr[1]=2, double → arr[1]=4, array: [2,4,3]. i=2: arr[2]=3, double → arr[2]=6, array: [2,4,6]. Final array: [2,4,6].

Array: [5,10,15]. Loop i from 0 to 2. i=0: arr[0]=5, not >10 → unchanged, array: [5,10,15]. i=1: arr[1]=10, not >10 → unchanged. i=2: arr[2]=15, >10 → replace with 0, arr[2]=0, array: [5,10,0]. Final array: [5,10,0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Assigning Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Perform fundamental array operations: read and write values at specific positions.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using indices to retrieve and modify elements in an array. Intuition: An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible via an index (usually starting at 0). Reading an element means getting its value at a given index, while writing means setting a new value at that index. These operations are constant time O(1) and are the building blocks for more complex algorithms. Step-by-step: To read: value = array[index]. To write: array[index] = newValue. Ensure the index is within bounds (0 to length-1) to avoid errors. This is essential for in-place modifications where we overwrite elements without extra space. Connection to L3: These operations are used within loops to traverse and update arrays. Connection to L5: It assumes understanding of arrays and indices. Common mistakes include using invalid indices (e.g., negative or >= length), confusing index with value, or not updating correctly. Real code pattern: # Reading: x = arr[i]; # Writing: arr[i] = y.

examples.input: Array [10,20,30], read index 1, write index 2 with 40
examples.trace: Initial array: [10,20,30]. Read index 1: arr[1] = 20. Write index 2 with 40: arr[2] = 40. Array becomes [10,20,40].

examples.input: Array [5,6,7], read index 0, write index 1 with value from index 2
examples.trace: Initial array: [5,6,7]. Read index 0: arr[0] = 5. Write index 1 with value from index 2: arr[1] = arr[2] = 7. Array becomes [5,7,7].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of arrays: what they are, how to create them, and use indices to refer to elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers arrays, a fundamental data structure in programming. An array is an ordered collection of elements, all of the same type, stored in contiguous memory. Each element has an index, starting from 0, which acts as its position. For example, in array [10, 20, 30], index 0 holds 10, index 1 holds 20, and index 2 holds 30. Intuition: Arrays allow efficient access to elements by position, making them ideal for problems involving sequences or lists. To use an array, you need to know its length (number of elements) and how to reference elements via indices. Creating an array involves initializing it with values. Basic operations include declaring an array, accessing an element by index (e.g., arr[i]), and understanding that indices must be within 0 to length-1. This concept is prerequisite for any array manipulation algorithm. Step-by-step: 1. Define an array: e.g., arr = [1,2,3]. 2. Get length: n = len(arr). 3. Access element at index i: value = arr[i]. 4. Ensure i is valid (0 <= i < n). Time and space: Access is O(1), and arrays have O(n) space for n elements. Connection to L4: This knowledge enables reading and writing array elements. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (thinking index 1 is first element), accessing out-of-bounds indices, or confusing arrays with other data structures. Real code pattern: # Create array: my_array = [5, 10, 15]; # Access: first = my_array[0]; # Length: size = len(my_array).

examples.input: Array [3,7,2], explain indices and access index 1
examples.trace: Array: [3,7,2]. It has 3 elements. Index 0 corresponds to value 3. Index 1 corresponds to value 7. Index 2 corresponds to value 2. To access index 1, we get arr[1] = 7.

examples.input: Array [100,200,300,400], find value at index 2 and index 0

approaches.name: Hash Set O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array, storing unique elements in a hash set, then copy them back to the array. This works for unsorted arrays but uses extra space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove Duplicates Using Hash Set
ladders.desc: Complete in-place removal of duplicates from a sorted array using a hash set for uniqueness tracking.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that hash sets provide O(1) average-time membership testing and insertion, making them ideal for tracking unique elements. Since we need to modify the array in-place, we can't simply return a new array with unique elements. Instead, we use the hash set to identify unique elements as we iterate through the original array, then overwrite the beginning of the array with these unique elements. This approach works for both sorted and unsorted arrays, though it's less efficient than two-pointer methods for sorted arrays. The algorithm proceeds as follows: 1) Initialize an empty hash set to track seen elements. 2) Initialize a write index to track where to place the next unique element. 3) Iterate through each element in the array. 4) For each element, check if it's in the hash set. 5) If not in the set, add it to the set and copy it to the write index position, then increment the write index. 6) If already in the set, skip it (it's a duplicate). 7) After processing all elements, the first 'write index' elements contain all unique elements in original order. The time complexity is O(n) since we make one pass through the array with O(1) hash set operations. Space complexity is O(n) for the hash set in worst case (all elements unique). This connects to the broader problem by providing a complete solution that handles any array (sorted or not) while maintaining the in-place requirement. Common mistakes include: forgetting to increment write index after copying, not handling empty arrays, or trying to return the hash set instead of modifying the array. Real code pattern: def remove_duplicates(arr): seen = set(); write_idx = 0; for num in arr: if num not in seen: seen.add(num); arr[write_idx] = num; write_idx += 1; return write_idx

examples.trace:
Initial: arr=[1,1,2,2,3,4,4,5], seen={}, write_idx=0
Step1: num=1, not in seen → seen={1}, arr[0]=1, write_idx=1, arr=[1,1,2,2,3,4,4,5]
Step2: num=1, in seen → skip, arr=[1,1,2,2,3,4,4,5]
Step3: num=2, not in seen → seen={1,2}, arr[1]=2, write_idx=2, arr=[1,2,2,2,3,4,4,5]
Step4: num=2, in seen → skip, arr=[1,2,2,2,3,4,4,5]
Step5: num=3, not in seen → seen={1,2,3}, arr[2]=3, write_idx=3, arr=[1,2,3,2,3,4,4,5]
Step6: num=4, not in seen → seen={1,2,3,4}, arr[3]=4, write_idx=4, arr=[1,2,3,4,3,4,4,5]
Step7: num=4, in seen → skip, arr=[1,2,3,4,3,4,4,5]
Step8: num=5, not in seen → seen={1,2,3,4,5}, arr[4]=5, write_idx=5, arr=[1,2,3,4,5,4,4,5]
Final: write_idx=5, first 5 elements are [1,2,3,4,5]

Initial: arr=[0,0,0,0], seen={}, write_idx=0
Step1: num=0, not in seen → seen={0}, arr[0]=0, write_idx=1, arr=[0,0,0,0]
Step2: num=0, in seen → skip, arr=[0,0,0,0]
Step3: num=0, in seen → skip, arr=[0,0,0,0]
Step4: num=0, in seen → skip, arr=[0,0,0,0]
Final: write_idx=1, first element is [0]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Unique Element Collection with Hash Set
ladders.desc: Extract unique elements from an array using a hash set while preserving order.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that hash sets automatically handle duplicates by only storing unique elements, but they don't preserve insertion order in all implementations. However, by iterating through the original array and adding elements to the set only when first encountered, we can maintain the original order of first occurrences. This sub-routine is the core of the full solution because it identifies which elements should be kept. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty hash set. 2) Initialize an empty list for unique elements (in practice, we'd write directly to array in full solution). 3) Iterate through each element in the input array. 4) For each element, check if it's in the hash set. 5) If not, add it to both the hash set and the unique list. 6) If yes, skip it. 7) Return the unique list. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration with O(1) hash set operations. Space complexity is O(n) for both the hash set and unique list in worst case. This feeds into L0 by providing the logic to identify unique elements; in L0 we modify this to write directly to the input array instead of creating a new list. Common mistakes include using a set without order preservation (some languages don't guarantee order), or incorrectly checking membership. Real code pattern: def get_unique(arr): seen = set(); unique = []; for num in arr: if num not in seen: seen.add(num); unique.append(num); return unique

examples.trace:
Initial: arr=[1,2,2,3,3,3], seen={}, unique=[]
Step1: num=1, not in seen → seen={1}, unique=[1]
Step2: num=2, not in seen → seen={1,2}, unique=[1,2]
Step3: num=2, in seen → skip
Step4: num=3, not in seen → seen={1,2,3}, unique=[1,2,3]
Step5: num=3, in seen → skip
Step6: num=3, in seen → skip
Final: unique=[1,2,3]

Initial: arr=[5,5,5], seen={}, unique=[]
Step1: num=5, not in seen → seen={5}, unique=[5]
Step2: num=5, in seen → skip
Step3: num=5, in seen → skip
Final: unique=[5]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Hash Set Membership Tracking
ladders.desc: Use a hash set to track seen elements and determine uniqueness during iteration.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that hash sets provide O(1) average-time operations for adding elements and checking membership, making them perfect for tracking which elements we've already encountered. The technique involves maintaining a set as we iterate through a collection: before processing an element, we check if it's in the set; if not, we add it and process it as unique; if yes, we skip it as a duplicate. This works because sets only store unique elements and offer constant-time lookups. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty hash set. 2) For each element in input sequence: a) Check if element in set. b) If not, add to set and mark as unique. c) If yes, mark as duplicate. 3) The set ends up containing all unique elements encountered. Time complexity is O(n) for n elements with O(1) set operations. Space complexity is O(k) where k is number of unique elements. This is the core of L1 because it's the mechanism for identifying unique elements; L1 builds on this by collecting the unique elements in order. Common mistakes include not initializing the set, or confusing set operations with list operations. Real code pattern: seen = set(); for item in collection: if item not in seen: seen.add(item); # Process as unique else: # Process as duplicate

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating with Conditional Logic
ladders.desc: Process each element in a collection with conditional checks and actions.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing a sequence element by element and performing different actions based on conditions. It's fundamental to many algorithms where decisions must be made during iteration. The intuition is that by examining elements one at a time, we can apply logic that depends on the element's properties or relationship to previously seen elements. The algorithm: 1) Start at the beginning of the collection. 2) For each element: a) Evaluate a condition (e.g., check if element meets some criterion). b) If condition true, perform action A. c) If condition false, perform action B (or skip). 3) Continue until all elements processed. Time complexity is O(n) for linear traversal. Space complexity is O(1) if not storing additional data. This builds into L2 by providing the iteration framework; L2 adds the specific condition of checking set membership. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loops, or not handling empty collections. Real code pattern: for element in collection: if condition(element): # Do something else: # Do something else

examples.trace:
Step1: element=3, condition 3>5 is false → skip
Step2: element=7, condition 7>5 is true → process
Step3: element=2, condition 2>5 is false → skip
Step4: element=8, condition 8>5 is true → process
Final: Processed elements: 7 and 8

examples.input: Collection: [a, b, a], condition: not seen before
examples.output: Process: a not seen process, b not seen process, a seen skip
examples.trace:
Assume tracking seen elements
Step1: element=a, not seen before → process, mark seen
Step2: element=b, not seen before → process, mark seen
Step3: element=a, seen before → skip
Final: Processed elements: a and b

testCases.expected: Process: A process, B skip, C process
Process: X process, X skip, Y process

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Hash Set Insertion and Lookup
ladders.desc: Add elements to a hash set and check for membership.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using a hash set's core functions: adding elements with add() and checking membership with in operator. Hash sets are data structures that store unique elements using hash tables, providing average O(1) time for these operations. The intuition is that when you add an element, it's placed in a bucket based on its hash value; checking membership computes the hash and looks in that bucket. The algorithm for tracking uniqueness: 1) Initialize empty set. 2) To check if element exists: use 'element in set' which returns boolean. 3) To add element if not present: use 'set.add(element)' which does nothing if already present. Time complexity is O(1) average case for both operations. Space complexity is O(n) for storing n unique elements. This is used in L3 as the condition check; L3 builds on this by incorporating it into iteration. Common mistakes include confusing set with list operations, or assuming order preservation. Real code pattern: my_set = set(); my_set.add(5); exists = 5 in my_set

examples.input: Operations: add 1, check 1, add 2, check 1, add 1
Operations: add apple, check banana, add apple

testCases.input: add A, add B, check A
add red, check red, add blue
add X, check Y, add Y

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Collections and Elements
ladders.desc: Understand arrays/lists as ordered collections of elements.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a collection (like an array or list) is a data structure that holds multiple elements in a specific order. Each element has a position (index) and a value. The intuition is that collections allow us to group related data together and process them systematically. Key properties: 1) Ordered: Elements have positions (first, second, etc.). 2) Indexable: Can access elements by position (e.g., arr[0] for first element). 3) Iterable: Can loop through elements one by one. 4) May allow duplicates: Same value can appear multiple times. For example, an array [1, 2, 1] has three elements: first is 1, second is 2, third is 1. This foundation is essential for L4 because hash sets operate on elements from collections. Common beginner mistakes include confusing indices (starting at 0 vs 1), or not understanding that collections can hold different types of elements. Real code pattern: my_list = [1, 2, 3]; first_element = my_list[0]; for element in my_list: print(element)

examples.trace:
Collection has 3 elements in order:
Position 0: value 5
Position 1: value 10
Position 2: value 15
Iteration would process: 5, then 10, then 15

Collection has 2 elements:
Index 0: 'apple'
Index 1: 'banana'
Accessing index 0 gives 'apple', index 1 gives 'banana'
